skill.name: libtv-skill
skill.description: Use LibTV and LiblibAI to create, edit, query, upload, and download AI-generated images and videos through authenticated OpenAPI sessions.
skill.summary: Create and manage LibTV image and video generation workflows from AI assistant sessions.
skill.author: libtv-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified network, environment, filesystem, and command-execution indicators, but most high-severity crypto and obfuscation alerts are false positives from documentation text. The real risk is medium: this skill sends prompts, credentials, and selected media to LibTV APIs, and its upload and download helpers need user-visible warnings and safer path and file validation.

medium_findings.title: External API Calls Use Bearer Credentials
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The scripts read LIBTV_ACCESS_KEY and send it in Authorization headers to the configured LibTV API base. This is expected for the connector, but it exposes a sensitive credential to a third-party service and to any overridden base URL.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential flow is explicit and semantically required by the service integration. I found no hidden destination beyond the configurable LibTV base URL, so this is elevated but not confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Media Upload To Third-Party Service
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: upload_file.py reads a local file and uploads it to the LibTV API. This matches the skill purpose, but users must understand that selected image or video content leaves the local machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload path is clear: a local file is opened, read, and submitted through urllib. The behavior is intentional for reference media, but it is a real privacy and data transfer risk.

medium_findings.title: Weak Local File Validation For Uploads
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The upload script checks MIME type with filename-based guessing and does not enforce the documented 200 MB limit before reading the file. This can allow unintended file contents or large files to be uploaded when a path is supplied.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses mimetypes.guess_type and reads the full file into memory. Legitimate use is likely, but the missing content and size checks are directly visible.

medium_findings.title: Download Path Can Be Influenced By User Arguments
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: download_results.py builds output paths from user-controlled output directory and prefix arguments. A prefix containing path separators could write outside the intended output directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path construction is visible and there is no prefix sanitization. Exploitation depends on a malicious or careless caller supplying unsafe arguments.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Expected Skill Usage
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The external command detections in SKILL.md are fenced usage examples that tell the assistant how to run the bundled Python scripts. They are not hidden shell execution inside code, but users should still review script behavior before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are human-readable documentation and command examples. They intentionally invoke local scripts, with no evidence of obfuscated or unexpected shell code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Appear To Be Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged weak cryptography lines are argparse help text, examples, or documentation rather than hashing or encryption logic. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic operations in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic implementation at the flagged locations. The likely triggers are ordinary text and model or example names, not security-sensitive algorithms.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Heuristic Is Human-Readable Markdown
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: SKILL.md is readable Markdown documentation with Chinese text, examples, URLs, and JSON snippets. No evidence found of encoded payloads, encrypted blobs, or binary content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file content is visible prose and examples, not obfuscated code. The entropy score is plausibly caused by multilingual text, identifiers, and URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: External API Calls Use Bearer Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The scripts read LIBTV_ACCESS_KEY and send it in Authorization headers to the configured LibTV API base. This is expected for the connector, but it exposes a sensitive credential to a third-party service and to any overridden base URL.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential flow is explicit and semantically required by the service integration. I found no hidden destination beyond the configurable LibTV base URL, so this is elevated but not confirmed malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Media Upload To Third-Party Service
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: upload_file.py reads a local file and uploads it to the LibTV API. This matches the skill purpose, but users must understand that selected image or video content leaves the local machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload path is clear: a local file is opened, read, and submitted through urllib. The behavior is intentional for reference media, but it is a real privacy and data transfer risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Download Path Can Be Influenced By User Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE POSITIVE: download_results.py builds output paths from user-controlled output directory and prefix arguments. A prefix containing path separators could write outside the intended output directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction is visible and there is no prefix sanitization. Exploitation depends on a malicious or careless caller supplying unsafe arguments.

content.value_statement: Creative teams need a simple way to send image and video requests to LibTV from an AI assistant. This skill creates sessions, uploads references, polls progress, and downloads generated media.
actual_capabilities: Create new LibTV sessions and send natural language image or video requests.
Send follow-up messages to an existing LibTV session.
Query session progress and return project canvas links when available.
Upload local image or video references to the LibTV upload endpoint.
Download generated image and video results from session messages or supplied URLs.
Switch the LibTV project bound to the current access key.
limitations: Requires a valid LIBTV_ACCESS_KEY environment variable.
Sends prompts, uploaded media, and authentication data to LibTV services.
Generation speed, quality, and model selection depend on the LibTV backend.
Upload validation is filename-based and does not enforce the documented size limit.

use_cases.title: Create campaign video drafts
use_cases.description: Generate draft videos, product scenes, or promotional assets from concise creative requests.

use_cases.title: Edit reference media
use_cases.description: Upload an existing image or video and request object replacement, style changes, or scene adjustments.

use_cases.title: Build storyboard assets
use_cases.description: Create image grids, short scenes, or visual concepts for planning larger productions.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a simple image
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one new visual asset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use LibTV to create an image of a clean product display for a new skincare bottle.

prompt_templates.title: Create a short video
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a short generated clip.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use LibTV to generate a 10-second video of a city street at night with soft rain and cinematic lighting.

prompt_templates.title: Edit uploaded media
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that uses a local reference file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload my reference video and ask LibTV to replace the paper boats with white paper hearts while preserving camera motion.

prompt_templates.title: Manage a full creative session
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with generation, polling, and download.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a LibTV session for a nine-panel storyboard, poll until results are ready, then download the generated images with a storyboard prefix.

output_examples.input: Create a short product video for a wireless speaker on a desk.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a LibTV session, reports the session identifier, waits for generated media, then returns the result links and project canvas link.

output_examples.input: Use this local video and change the main prop color.
output_examples.output: The assistant uploads the video, sends the edit request with the uploaded reference link, polls progress, and downloads the finished media.

output_examples.input: Download all completed results from my session.
output_examples.output: The assistant extracts available image and video links from the session and saves the media files into the chosen output folder.

best_practices: Use only media files that you are allowed to upload to LibTV.
Keep the LibTV access key private and scoped to the intended account.
Use clear prompts, then let the LibTV backend handle detailed creative planning.
anti_patterns: Do not upload confidential or regulated media unless your LibTV account permits it.
Do not override the API base URL unless you control and trust the destination.
Do not use path separators in download filename prefixes.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to LibTV and LiblibAI OpenAPI endpoints for image and video generation workflows.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires LIBTV_ACCESS_KEY in the environment before the scripts can call the service.

faq.question: Can it upload reference files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can upload local image and video files for editing or reference-based generation.

faq.question: Where are generated files saved?
faq.answer: Downloaded results are saved to the requested output directory, or to a LibTV results folder under Downloads by default.

faq.question: Is the skill fully local?
faq.answer: No. It sends requests and selected media to LibTV services, then downloads generated results.

faq.question: What security warnings apply?
faq.answer: Review uploaded media carefully, protect the access key, and avoid unsafe download prefixes or untrusted API base URLs.
